coredata.dc:title: An extended single-particle model of lithium-ion batteries based on simplified solid-liquid diffusion process
coredata.dc:creator: He, Wei
Han, Tao
Song, Haiqin
Wang, Qian
Kang, Jianqiang
Wang, Jing V.
Chen, Weihua
coredata.dc:description: An extended lithium-ion battery model is proposed, which simplifies the solid-liquid diffusion process compared with the full-order pseudo two-dimensional (P2D) model, in order to reduce computational complexity and enhance modeling speed. To simplify the model, the three-parameter method is utilized to simplify the solid-phase diffusion process. Meanwhile, exponential fitting is applied to simplify the liquid-phase diffusion process. In addition, the average current flux is used to simplify the current exchange density at the solid-liquid interface. Finally, experiments are performed to verify the model, and experimental results demonstrate that, even though fewer parameters need to be identified, the simplified model still has satisfied accuracy under constant current and dynamic situations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemistry
Electrochemical energy storage
Computational chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Comparison of P2D model and ESP model
Experimental validation of the ESP model

This paper is funded by the research project from Three Gorges Electric Energy Co., Ltd., Wuhan, Hubei, 430000, China, the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grants No. 52277224 and National Key Laboratory of Electromagnetic Energy under Grants No. 61422172320104.

head.ce:title: An extended single-particle model of lithium-ion batteries based on simplified solid-liquid diffusion process

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Three Gorges Electric Energy Co., Ltd., Wuhan, Hubei 430000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry & Green Catalysis Center, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An extended lithium-ion battery model is proposed, which simplifies the solid-liquid diffusion process compared with the full-order pseudo two-dimensional (P2D) model, in order to reduce computational complexity and enhance modeling speed. To simplify the model, the three-parameter method is utilized to simplify the solid-phase diffusion process. Meanwhile, exponential fitting is applied to simplify the liquid-phase diffusion process. In addition, the average current flux is used to simplify the current exchange density at the solid-liquid interface. Finally, experiments are performed to verify the model, and experimental results demonstrate that, even though fewer parameters need to be identified, the simplified model still has satisfied accuracy under constant current and dynamic situations.
Chemistry; Electrochemical energy storage; Computational chemistry

ce:section.ce:para:
Lithium-ion batteries have been widely used in portable electronic devices, automobiles, and energy storage fields, among others, due to their advantages of high energy, high power density, and long life. 1
,
2 The lithium-ion battery model is one of the components of the electric vehicle battery management system (BMS), and an accurate battery model can better estimate the current state of the battery system in real time. Currently, Rint models, Thevenin models, and second-order RC models are among the battery models that are frequently utilized in BMS. These models are essentially analogous circuit models made up of electronic components like resistors, inductors, and capacitors. This kind of model can satisfy the requirements of some application scenarios due to its straightforward structure, minimal computation load, and excellent accuracy. Nevertheless, it is challenging to explain the sophisticated physical and chemical processes inside the battery using this model, which can only partially reflect the exterior characteristics of the battery.

Electrochemical models, on the other hand, use a set of complicated partial differential equations to represent intricate internal reaction processes. A common electrochemical model is the pseudo two-dimensional (P2D) model, which was suggested by Doyle 3 and is based on concentration theory and porous electrode theory. Unfortunately, this model is extremely complex, computationally demanding, and challenging to use in practice. To decrease its complexity, several simpler models have been presented by researchers. 4
,
5 The single-particle (SP) electrochemical model is one of the most studied. For example, the P2D model was made much simpler by Xu’s SP model, which made the assumption that the concentration of lithium ions outside the particle is uniform. In order to further increase calculation accuracy, the author also took into account variations in the distribution of lithium ions within the particles and described the particle state using the finite difference approach. 6
,
7 Some scholars have assumed that the concentration of lithium ions in the electrode particles is linearly distributed, and the concentration distribution is simplified by equation fitting. 8 Ting’s simulation of the diffusion process of lithium-ion solid phase was done roughly using a three-parameter parabolic equation. 9 Compared with the finite difference method, the computational complexity is significantly reduced and the computational accuracy is improved. This approach, however, adds three more unknown parameters and makes the task of identifying the remaining parameters more difficult. A quartic polynomial approximation was used by Subramanian et al. 10 to simplify the solid diffusion process, eliminating the need for unknown parameters and lowering computing complexity. In addition, in situations of high charging/discharging rate, they can also obtain more precise numerical solutions.

While the SP model and its extended models are capable of accurately simulating battery charging and discharging behavior at low current rate, the error will increase as the current rate increases because the difference in the liquid phase potential distribution cannot be ignored. Known as the simplified P2D (SP2D), Han 11 suggested a multi-particle model with four particles equally distributed at the positive and negative poles. The solid-phase diffusion process was made simpler by SP2D by a two-state model. However, the solid-liquid interface exchange current density calculation was excessively complex and the liquid phase diffusion calculation was not convergent when the battery’s characteristics were not exact. Similarly, Bi 12 established an electrochemical model with 13 negative particles and a single positive particle and studied the estimation of battery health status under the assumption of negative electrode aging. Although the calculation result of the model is accurate, the calculation of the negative electrode is too complex. 13 The electrolyte resistance value was added to the model to improve the accuracy of the SP model. The influence of the liquid phase lithium ionization concentration distribution on the liquid phase potential is further considered, and the accuracy of the model is improved at a higher magnification. 14 Fan 15 converts the partial differential equations of solid-liquid phase diffusion in the P2D model into polynomials and ordinary differential equations, which also reduces the computational complexity.

Due to the large number of model parameters in the aforementioned method, the computational workload is still high. The simplified model proposed in this paper further reduces the computational workload and improves the computational speed. The solid-liquid phase of the model is simplified based on the P2D model. Simultaneously, the exchange current density is made simpler by using the average current flow. As taking into account the influence of liquid phase potential, the model’s convergence is enhanced. On the other hand, the exchange current density calculation is made simpler as compared to the SP2D model, improving the accuracy of the high-rate discharge calculation. In order to reduce the number of parameter identifications, we study the sensitivity of the model’s parameters and acquire them through battery disassembly measurement, reference, and parameter identification method. The computation speed is clearly increased when comparing the P2D model with the simplified model. Furthermore, a comparison of simulation results based on the simplified model and experimental results based on tests of real batteries demonstrates the great fitting precision of the model.

P2D model described a battery made up of a positive electrode, a separator, and a negative electrode. The porous active material particles, which can be roughly described as spherical particles, fill the positive and negative electrodes. As seen in Figure 1 , the pores of the particles are filled with electrolytes, and a porous separator separates the positive and negative electrodes to prevent a short circuit between them.

At the microscale of positively and negatively polarized active material particles, lithium ions diffuse within spherical particles according to Fick’s second law, moving along the radial direction (r) of the spheres. In the battery’s macroscopic thickness in the x direction, lithium ions are believed to move along the x direction in the electrolyte, driven by concentration and electric fields. Besides, positive and negative electrode particles’ surfaces undergo interfacial chemical reactions. During charging, lithium ions detach from the surface of positive electrode particles, move into the electrolyte, cross through the separator, enter the negative electrode, and embed themselves in the particles. Conversely, during discharge, the reverse occurs. This reaction process can be defined using the following partial differential equations.

Numerous coupled partial differential equations are present in the original lithium-ion battery P2D. These equations must be simplified in order to decrease computing complexity and increase calculation performance.

Due to the approximate parabolic distribution of lithium-ion concentration inside solid-phase particles along the particle radius direction, a two-state equation was used in the study by Ting et al. 9 to fit such a distribution curve, thereby obtaining an approximate lithium-ion concentration on the particle surface. Although this method provides a more accurate approximation of the lithium-ion concentration distribution compared to the fourth-order polynomial fitting method, it adds four additional parameters that need to be identified. These four parameters require experimental data to fit the concentration distribution inside the particles, making them difficult to obtain. In order to reduce the number of parameter identification and ensure a certain level of computational accuracy, this paper adopts a three-parameter parabolic fitting method to approximate the solid phase diffusion process. 10
The fourth-order polynomial simplification process of solid state diffusion is as follows:

Electrode solid phase diffusion satisfies Fick’s second law:

is the average lithium concentration,

is the average charge flux, Equation 9 is the equation for calculating the average lithium concentration of particles, Equation 10 is the equation for calculating the lithium concentration on the surface of particles, and Equation 11 is the equation for calculating the average lithium-ion flux inside the particles. The initial SP model displays a relatively big error in high charging/discharging rate, primarily because it disregards the impact of lithium-ion liquid-phase distribution on terminal voltage. To mitigate this issue, this article investigates the impact of liquid-phase lithium-ion distribution based on the SP model.

Liquid lithium-ion concentration in electrochemical models is often time- and position-dependent. Two physical processes determine the concentration of liquid lithium ions in the positive and negative regions: the first process is the diffusion motion based on concentration difference (Fick’s law), and the other one is the motion of solid lithium ions in the positive and negative regions. The gradient diffusion process of Fick’s theorem is the only process that occurs in the diaphragm region because there is no solid-liquid lithium-ion exchange.

In order to simplify the liquid-phase diffusion process, Luo et al. 16 approximated the concentration of lithium ions in the liquid phase of the battery, using an exponential fitting to approximate the distribution of lithium ions in the positive and negative polar regions. The local exchange current density j
f
(x,t) was approximated by the average current flux I(t)/A and was decoupled. Combined with boundary conditions, the distribution of lithium-ion concentration in the liquid phase in Table 1 can be simplified using Equation 25.
(Equation 25)

To determine the terminal voltage, it is essential to resolve the liquid lithium-ion concentration at the interface of the positive and negative electrodes and the collector. Equations 27 and 28 denote the estimated solution for the lithium-ion concentration at the boundary, while Equation 29 illustrates the liquid-phase potential difference.
(Equation 27)

p are explained in detail in the study by Liu et al. 17

The exchange current bulk density of the battery is determined by the Bulter-Volmer equation in Table 1, which dissociated from the solid-liquid phase’s surface overpotential and was simplified in a more involved manner in the study by Ting et al. and Fan. 9
,
15 This will have an effect on the aging modeling of batteries, but the calculation process is rather laborious and the logarithmic operation of the model is prone to errors in the calculation process, especially after some parameters change slightly. In order to simplify the exchange current density, we add the average current bulk density from Equations 21 and 22 to the electrochemical polarization overpotential expression. This results in the overpotential expression being equal to Equation 30.
(Equation 30)
d
q ¯
( t )
d t
+
30
D s
q ¯

The battery output voltage expression of the simplified electrochemical model can be expressed as:

18650 Type 811NMC ternary lithium battery manufactured by Pengcheng New Energy Technology Co., Ltd. is used in the present paper as the subject of modeling and testing. The battery has a rated capacity of 2,500 mAh and a rated voltage range of 2.7–4.2 V. Considering the battery model includes numerous parameters, some of which are challenging to obtain, these parameters are classified into different categories according to acquisition difficulty.
The first category of parameters is size and thermodynamic parameters. They can be acquired through cell disassembly measurements, as demonstrated in Table 2 .

In order to identify the lithium intercalation range of the battery and the corresponding positive and negative electrode capacity within the range, we need to obtain the positive and negative electrode balance potential of the battery and the actual balance potential of the entire battery. The full battery data were obtained through constant current charging and discharging of a 18650 cylindrical ternary lithium battery at 0.02C in the laboratory and measured in a constant temperature box at 25°C. The low-rate charging and discharging process can approximately ignore the impact of various polarization phenomena inside the battery.

Owing to restrictions in the laboratory environment, measuring the equilibrium potential of the positive and negative electrodes is challenging. Equations 33 and 34 exhibit the values retrieved from sources. 16
,
17 The equilibrium potential diagram is shown in Figures 2 and 3 .
(Equation 33)
U p

The matching relationship between the balance potential of the positive and negative electrodes and the balance potential of the whole cell is shown in Equations 35, 36, and 37

Among them, p
0/100 and n 0/100 are the upper and lower limits of the lithium intercalation range for the positive and negative electrodes, respectively. “0” and “100” represent the state where the SOC of the entire battery is 0% and 100%, respectively. Q
p and Q
n are the positive and negative electrode capacities, and R is the DC equivalent internal resistance. For this relatively simple parameter optimization problem, the identification method uses the random gradient descent method to quickly obtain the parameters to be identified, and the loss function is shown in Equation 38:
(Equation 38)
min
(
V −

e x
)
2
/ N
In the formula, V is the battery voltage reconstructed from the balance potential of the positive and negative electrodes, V
ex is the experimental battery voltage, and N is the number of sampling points. The fitting results are shown in Figures 4 and 5 :
Table 3 displays the upper and lower limit of the identified lithium intercalation range.

Among them, SOC
p100 and SOC
n100 are the upper limits of the lithium intercalation interval of positive and negative electrodes and SOC
p100 and SOC
p100 are the lower limits. “0” and “100” represent the state of SOC of 0% and 100%, respectively.

The second category of parameters are challenging to obtain, thus a parameter sensitivity analysis is utilized to classify them further. Using COSMOL multi-physics software, perform parameter scanning on the parameters, that is, by using the control variable method to take different values of these parameters for simulation, in order to analyze their impact on the output voltage. Due to the numerous parameters of the electrochemical model, it is not convenient to display the scanning results of all parameters. Therefore, Figure 6 only shows the scanning results of four parameters.
( a )
( b )
( c )
( d )

To further quantify the effect of each parameter on the voltage, we introduced the mean absolute difference (MAD
v ) of the voltage curve to describe the degree of difference between each set of curves, that is, the sensitivity of the model to each parameter (Figure 7 ); the calculation formula is shown as Equation 39.
(Equation 39)
M A
D v
=
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
|
(
V i max
−
V i min
)
−
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
(
V i max
−
V i min
)
|
According to the results of sensitivity analysis, the aforementioned parameters were classified, and the low- and middle-sensitive parameters were obtained by reference, as shown in Table 4 .

Through the sensitivity analysis of the aforementioned parameters, we will identify the parameters with medium to high sensitivity. By fitting the simplified electrochemical model with the experimental battery discharge data, we can identify the parameters we need. The objective function of the optimization problem is the same as Equation 38. The identification condition adopts a dynamic condition that includes 0.5C, 1C, 1.5C, and 2C charging and discharging pulses. The discharge current condition is shown in Figure 8 .

The battery’s discharge curve is significantly impacted by the very sensitive characteristics. By using the DST condition identification, we were able to determine which parameters were highly sensitive, and Table 5 displays these parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of P2D model and ESP model
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the accuracy of the proposed ESP, we established a P2D model in finite element simulation software by using parameters from the study by Safari and Delacourt. 21 The P2D model was used as a real battery to verify the effectiveness of the simplified model. At the same time, a simplified diffusion SP model (ESP) was also established in MATLAB. The comparison of positive and negative electrode SOC between the two models under 1C rate discharge is shown in Figures 9 and 10 . The positive and negative electrode SOC at the boundary has a direct impact on the terminal voltage of the battery. It can be seen that the positive and negative electrode SOC of the ESP model can fit well with the P2D model, which also demonstrates the rationality of solid-phase simplification.

The potential of the liquid phase and the superpotential at the solid-liquid interface are presented in Figures 11 and 12 . The concentration distribution of lithium ions in the liquid phase is simplified to a certain extent by the ESP model, which may result in some errors. However, these errors are deemed acceptable. The superpotential of the solid-liquid interface is determined by the potential of the solid phase, the potential of the liquid phase, and the equilibrium potential, which may result in some inaccuracies.

While the accuracy of the model can be guaranteed, the calculation time can also be increased by rationally simplifying the model. Table 6 displays the two models’ computation times under 1C discharge conditions.

Under both dynamic DST conditions and constant current discharge conditions of 0.5C, 1C, 1.5C, and 2C, the efficacy of the simplified model was confirmed. Figures 13 and 14 display the terminal voltage outputs of the two models, while Figures 15 and 16 display the relative errors of each condition:

It is evident from Figures 13 and 14 that, in both dynamic and constant current scenarios, the terminal voltage output of the simplified model may fit the voltage output of the P2D model quite well. The ESP model’s inaccuracy rises with increasing current ratio, yet it still stays within a narrow range. Table 7 displays the terminal voltage error between ESP and P2D model, including the maximum error (ME), average error (AE), and root-mean-square error (RMSE).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental validation of the ESP model
ce:section.ce:para: We substitute the identified parameters into the model to calculate the voltage output under various operating conditions, and compare it with the experimental voltage of batteries under test. The charging and discharging curves of the model and experimental battery under constant current and dynamic operating conditions are shown in Figures 17 , 18 , and 19 :

The discrepancy between the ESP model voltage curves and the experimental curves under both constant and dynamic conditions is displayed in Figures 20 , 21 , and 22 . Under constant current conditions, the error is higher toward the end of discharge, but the overall error is low. Additionally, the overall error of the ESP model under dynamic conditions is low with the maximum error being less than 0.09 V. Table 8 demonstrates that the RMSE of the ESP model is negligible across all conditions, indicating high simulation accuracy.

In this paper, the solid-liquid interface diffusion, liquid-phase diffusion, and exchange current density equations of the lithium-ion battery P2D model are all simplified. Firstly, the solid phase is simplified using a three-parameter fitting method. The liquid phase is simplified using the exponential fitting method, and the exchange current density of the solid-liquid interface is simplified using average current flux. By comparing the simulation results of the P2D model and the ESP model, the rationality of the ESP model is verified, and the simulation time is shorter and the calculation speed is faster. Through experimental results and comparison with the ESP model, it is found that the maximum RMSE under constant current conditions is 12.65 mV; under dynamic conditions, it displays 13.90 mV, verifying the accuracy of the ESP model.
This paper does not explore the aging mechanisms at different SOC levels under various temperatures. This will be the focus of our future works.
Further information and requests for resources and reagents should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Haiqin Song (2389053576@qq.com).
This study did not generate new unique reagents.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data reported in this paper will be shared by the lead contact upon request.
This paper does not report original code.
Any additional information required to reanalyze the data reported in this paper is available from the lead contact upon request.

ce:section.ce:para: This paper is funded by the research project from Three Gorges Electric Energy Co., Ltd., Wuhan, Hubei, 430000, China, the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grants No. 52277224 and National Key Laboratory of Electromagnetic Energy under Grants No. 61422172320104.

W.H.: methodology, investigation, and supervision. T.H.: methodology, investigation, and writing – original draft. H.S.: supervision and writing – review and editing. Q.W.: supervision and writing – review and editing. J.K.: writing – review and editing and investigation. J.V.W.: conceptualization and writing – review and editing. W.C.: writing – review and editing.
The authors declare no competing interests.

In this paper, a simplified electrochemical model is proposed, which has the advantages of shorter simulation speed and faster calculation. The solid-liquid interface diffusion equation, the liquid-phase diffusion equation and the exchange current density equation of the P2D model for lithium-ion batteries are simplified. First, the solid phase was simplified using the three-parameter fitting method. The liquid phase was simplified with the exponential fitting method, and the exchange current density at the solid-liquid interface was simplified with the average current flow. The reasonableness of the ESP model was verified by comparing the P2D model and experiments.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid Lithium-Ion Battery Impedance Measurements Using Binary Sequence With Optimized Frequency Components
IEEE Trans. Ind. Electron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Meng J, Du X, Peng J, , Lin, M., Song, Z.. Rapid Lithium-Ion Battery Impedance Measurements Using Binary Sequence With Optimized Frequency Components. IEEE Trans. Ind. Electron. (Volume: 71,Issue: 7, July 2024), 7190-7198 ,DOI: 10.1109/TIE.2023.3306426.
